title: Multi-Protocol Access
description: Access the same data through MongoDB wire protocol and JavaScript. Prove protocol convergence — a MongoDB app can connect to Oracle without changing a single line of driver code.
introduction:
Protocol convergence is the final proof point. You've modeled data relationally, accessed it as documents through duality views, queried JSON collections with SQL, built single-table designs with multi-value indexes, and executed cross-model joins. All through SQL. Now you'll prove that the same data — the same tables, the same collections, the same indexes, the same optimizer — is accessible through entirely different protocols without any translation layer, any sync process, or any data duplication.



[svg:m5-protocols.svg]

ORDS (Oracle REST Data Services) implements the MongoDB wire protocol natively. That means mongosh — the official MongoDB shell — connects to Oracle and issues find(), insertOne(), deleteOne() against duality views and JSON collections as if they were MongoDB collections. This is not a compatibility shim that approximates MongoDB behavior. The wire protocol translates MongoDB operations into Oracle SQL internally, so every operation gets the cost-based optimizer, ACID transactions, and B-tree indexes. Your existing MongoDB application can point its connection string at Oracle and work without changing a single line of driver code. Duality views appear as collections, so an insertOne() through the MongoDB protocol actually writes to normalized relational tables through the bidirectional mapping.

On the JavaScript side, node-oracledb provides three distinct access patterns. First, SQL queries against duality views that return native JavaScript objects — no JSON.parse() required because Oracle's OSON binary format is deserialized natively by the driver. Second, JSON type binding (oracledb.DB_TYPE_JSON) that lets you insert JavaScript objects directly into JSON collections without manual serialization — Oracle converts them to binary OSON on ingest. Third, the SODA (Simple Oracle Document Access) API that provides a document-store interface with the same mental model as MongoDB's driver API but backed by Oracle's transactional engine.

All three access patterns — MongoDB wire protocol, SQL via node-oracledb, and SODA — hit the same data, the same indexes, the same optimizer. A document inserted through mongosh is immediately visible to a SQL query. A row inserted through node-oracledb is immediately queryable through the MongoDB protocol. There is no replication lag, no sync window, no eventual consistency gap. This is what convergence actually means: not one protocol to rule them all, but every protocol backed by one truth.
checkpoint: You just wrote to relational tables using the MongoDB wire protocol. A MongoDB application can connect to Oracle without changing a single line of driver code.

exercises.title: MongoDB API via mongosh
exercises.description: List collections, query documents, insert through MongoDB API, and verify via SQL.
exercises.explanation:
Protocol convergence is where the architecture thesis becomes undeniable. You have been querying this data with SQL for four modules. Now you will access the **exact same data** — same tables, same collections, same indexes, same ACID guarantees — through the MongoDB wire protocol without changing a single line of driver code. This is not a compatibility shim or an emulation layer. It is the same optimizer, the same transaction engine, just a different wire format.

[svg:m5-mongosh-flow.svg]

Oracle's **ORDS (Oracle REST Data Services)** implements the MongoDB wire protocol natively. **mongosh** — the official MongoDB shell — connects to Oracle and issues `find()`, `insertOne()`, `deleteOne()` against the same tables and collections you have been querying with SQL. The wire protocol translates MongoDB operations into Oracle SQL internally, so every `find()` becomes a SELECT with the cost-based optimizer choosing the execution plan, every `insertOne()` becomes an INSERT with full ACID durability, and every index you created in Module 3 is used automatically.

Duality views are where this gets powerful. They appear as MongoDB collections, so an `insertOne()` through mongosh actually writes to **normalized relational tables** via the duality view's bidirectional mapping — the document is decomposed into rows across advisors, clients, accounts, and holdings tables in a single transaction. The `show collections` command reveals both native JSON collections and duality views as MongoDB-compatible collections, giving existing MongoDB applications a migration path that requires zero application rewrites.

Key connection details: your connection string uses the ORDS endpoint URL with the MongoDB API path, and authentication maps to Oracle database credentials. The supported MongoDB operations include `find()`, `insertOne()`, `updateOne()`, `deleteOne()`, `countDocuments()`, `aggregate()`, and `createIndex()`. Unsupported operations return clear error messages rather than silent failures.

The tradeoff to understand: you get MongoDB API compatibility backed by Oracle's storage engine, but some MongoDB-specific features (like change streams and the `$lookup` pipeline stage) are not supported because they do not map cleanly to Oracle's architecture. What you gain is far more valuable — ACID transactions, the cost-based optimizer, relational joins when you need them, and a single source of truth instead of a polyglot persistence tax.

**Try this:** Run `db.CLIENT_INTERACTIONS.find({"sentiment": "positive"}).limit(5)` to filter by sentiment, or try `db.CLIENT_INTERACTIONS.countDocuments({"type": "call"})` to see MongoDB aggregation working against Oracle storage. You can also run `db.ADVISORY_ENTITIES.find({"entityType": "holding", "data.tags": "dividend"})` to query the single-table collection from Module 3 using MongoDB syntax.

steps.label: List all collections visible through MongoDB wire protocol
Query a duality view as a MongoDB collection
Insert through MongoDB API — writes to relational tables via duality view

exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM client_interactions ci WHERE ci.data.client_id.number() = 3
exercises.checkpoint: You just accessed Oracle through the MongoDB wire protocol. A MongoDB application can connect to Oracle without changing a single line of driver code. Same data, same ACID guarantees.

exercises.title: JavaScript with node-oracledb
exercises.description: Query duality views, insert with JSON type binding, and use the SODA API — all from JavaScript.
exercises.explanation:
The **node-oracledb** driver completes the protocol convergence story. You have accessed this data through SQL, through the MongoDB wire protocol, and now through JavaScript — three access patterns, same data, same indexes, same ACID guarantees. The driver provides three distinct patterns, and understanding when to use each one is the difference between a clean architecture and an accidental one.

[svg:m5-node-oracledb.svg]

First, **SQL queries against duality views** return native JavaScript objects. The JSON column maps directly to JS objects without any `JSON.parse()` call because Oracle's OSON binary format is deserialized natively by the driver. The key parameter is `outFormat: oracledb.OUT_FORMAT_OBJECT`, which tells the driver to return rows as JavaScript objects rather than positional arrays. This pattern gives you the full power of SQL — joins, aggregations, window functions — with a JavaScript-native result format.

Second, **JSON type binding** (`oracledb.DB_TYPE_JSON`) lets you insert JavaScript objects directly into JSON collections without manual serialization. The binding syntax `{ val: myObject, type: oracledb.DB_TYPE_JSON }` tells Oracle to convert the JavaScript object to binary OSON on ingest, which means O(1) hash-indexed field access on every subsequent read. No `JSON.stringify()`, no text parsing overhead, no encoding ambiguity. The object goes in as a native binary document.

Third, the **SODA (Simple Oracle Document Access)** API provides a document-store interface — `createCollection()`, `insertOne()`, `find().filter()` — with the same mental model as MongoDB's driver API but backed by Oracle's transactional engine. SODA is ideal when your application thinks in documents and you want to avoid writing SQL entirely.

The architectural tradeoff across these three patterns is about control versus convenience. SQL gives you maximum control — you write the query, you control the execution plan, you can join across tables and collections. JSON type binding gives you the fastest insert path for document workloads. SODA gives you the most MongoDB-like developer experience. All three hit the same storage engine, the same optimizer, the same transaction log. A document inserted through SODA is immediately visible to a SQL query. A row inserted through SQL is immediately queryable through SODA. There is no replication lag, no sync window, no eventual consistency gap. Same data. Same ACID. Multiple access paths.

**Try this:** After the duality view query, explore the nested structure with `console.log(JSON.stringify(clientDoc.accounts[0], null, 2))` to see account details, or try querying with a different client ID like `{ id: 1 }` to compare portfolio structures across clients.

steps.label: Query a duality view — returns native JavaScript objects
Insert a document via JSON type binding
Verify and clean up the test record

exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM client_portfolio_dv WHERE json_value(data, '$._id' RETURNING NUMBER) = 3
exercises.checkpoint: You just used three distinct JavaScript access patterns — SQL on duality views, JSON type binding, and SODA API — all hitting the same data, same indexes, same optimizer.
